region_of_origin: India (North Indian subcontinent); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Aloo Methi; closest defensible anchor is the generic 'potato dish' class. A more specific 'potato and fenugreek leaf curry' class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Aloo Methi. Code A0FJP (potato-based dish, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Fenugreek leaf component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Aloo Methi or a sufficiently close Indian vegetable curry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Aloo Methi is approximately 250 g (one bowl), providing around 320 kcal, 35 g carbohydrate, 18 g fat, and 6 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list a composite Aloo Methi entry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked potato (boiled/sautéed) and fresh fenugreek leaves, combined in an approximate 70:30 ratio by weight, with added cooking oil (~8 g/100 g dish) and salt. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, fibre, sodium, calcium, iron, and vitamin C values are consistent with the input record and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked potato (FDC ID 170026) and raw fenugreek leaves (FDC ID 170383). The input values (energy 128 kcal, protein 2.4 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 14.0 g) are retained as they fall within the defensible range for this dish composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Methi as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant ingredient being boiled/sautéed potato, which has a published GI of approximately 63–70 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The fenugreek leaf component is known to attenuate postprandial glucose response (Neelakantan et al., 2014, J Diabetes), which may lower the effective GI slightly below that of plain potato. A conservative estimate of GI 65 is applied. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (65 × 14.0 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 260 mg/100 g; estimated potassium ~390 mg/100 g provides partial DASH-diet offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced added salt during cooking to bring sodium below 200 mg/100 g, and should retain generous fenugreek leaves and potato portions to maximise the potassium-to-sodium ratio in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI 65 (medium); carbohydrate 14 g/100 g primarily from high-GI potato; fenugreek leaves provide partial hypoglycaemic offset
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with a high-protein or high-fibre accompaniment such as dal to blunt postprandial glucose response, and consider allowing potatoes to cool before eating to increase resistant starch content and lower effective GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat low at 1.2 g/100 g; cholesterol 0 mg/100 g; dietary fibre 2.4 g/100 g; sodium 260 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Methi is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; however, individuals with CVD should request reduced salt preparation to keep sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day limit, and should use a minimal amount of unsaturated cooking oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable; solanine content may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should consider substituting potato with sweet potato (non-nightshade) and retaining fenugreek leaves and turmeric for their anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Methi (Hindi: आलू मेथी) is a classic North Indian dry vegetable curry (sabzi) made with diced or cubed potatoes (aloo) and fresh fenugreek leaves (methi). The dish is prepared by sautéing cumin seeds, onion, ginger, and garlic in oil until fragrant, then adding the potatoes and spices — typically turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder — before folding in the fenugreek leaves and simmering until the potatoes are tender and the moisture has evaporated. The result is a dry, lightly spiced curry with a distinctive earthy, mildly bitter flavour from the fenugreek leaves balanced by the starchy sweetness of the potato. Aloo Methi is a staple home-cooked dish across North India and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 128 kcal, 14 g carbohydrate, 7.2 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil, with only 1.2 g saturated fat), 2.4 g protein, and 2.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 260 mg/100 g. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Fenugreek leaves are a notable source of iron (1.0 mg/100 g), calcium (32 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (10 mg/100 g), and have evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), driven primarily by the potato content. Clinically, the dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile, moderately suitable for hypertension if salt is reduced, and requires portion control for Type 2 Diabetes management. Nightshade-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the potato content.
common_user_queries: Is aloo methi good for diabetes?
Aloo methi for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked potato and fresh fenugreek leaves combined in an approximate ratio, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central. No single approved-source composite entry for Aloo Methi exists. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled/sautéed potato GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indian vegetable curry is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single dish entry as of 2024. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available as a single composite entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a plant-based dish cooked in vegetable oil with no hydrogenated fat. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. potassium_mg value of 390 mg/100 g is an estimate derived from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data for potato and fenugreek leaves; it is not a directly measured composite value. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained. The original input data_source was 'grabfood' with provenance 'ai_generated'; nutrient values have been cross-validated against approved sources and are within defensible range. Two portions in the input both labelled 'is_default: true' (1 bowl and 1 plate, both 250 g) — 1 bowl is adopted as the default serving for this record.
